title: DMZ Originated Sessions Hitting CDE Internal Segments

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run. Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757)
spl:
index=netfw sourcetype="pan:traffic" action=allowed earliest=-7d
| lookup pci_dmz_assets.csv ip AS src OUTPUT in_dmz
| lookup pci_cde_internal.csv ip AS dest OUTPUT in_cde_internal
| where in_dmz="true" AND in_cde_internal="true"
| bin _time span=1h
| stats count by _time, src, dest, app, dest_port
| eventstats perc95(count) as p95 globally
| where count>p95 AND p95>0
| sort - count
value: Detects allowed paths from DMZ assets into internal CDE networks that violate one-way DMZ design, reducing the chance a compromised web tier pivots into systems that store or process account data.
implementation: (1) Refresh DMZ and CDE internal inventories weekly from CMDB. (2) Validate expected admin SSH paths. (3) Create ES notable for sustained spikes. (4) Document compensating controls if architecture requires exceptions.
visualization: Line chart (hourly counts), bubble chart (src size by count), table (spike windows).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.app, All_Traffic.dest_port span=1h | sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks

knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
